children.name: Numenius
children.description: Curlews — large, long-billed waders whose haunting, liquid calls are among the most evocative sounds in the natural world. The Eurasian curlew is one of Sweden's most recognisable waders, its long decurved bill perfectly adapted for probing into mud, peat, and grass root zones to extract earthworms, insects, and crustaceans. The bubbling song of the curlew, delivered in soaring display flight over boggy farmland and coastal meadows, is the defining sound of the Swedish countryside in spring — but it is a sound that is becoming scarcer as agricultural intensification and drainage destroy the wet grasslands the curlew requires for nesting.

children.name: Limosa
children.description: Godwits — large waders with long, straight or slightly upturned bills, named from the Old English for 'good' and a word for their cry. Four species constitute this genus, and all are renowned for extraordinary long-distance migrations. The bar-tailed godwit holds the world record for non-stop bird flight, covering over 12,000 km from Alaska to New Zealand without landing. In breeding plumage godwits are strikingly coloured in rich chestnut and brick red; in winter they adopt plain grey-brown tones. Several species have experienced steep population declines due to loss of intertidal habitats at staging sites.

children.name: Gallinago
children.description: Snipe — cryptically streaked marsh birds whose remarkable aerial displays produce one of the most unusual sounds in the animal kingdom. The common snipe is a master of camouflage, its brown-and-buff streaked plumage rendering it almost invisible among sedge and rush as it stands motionless. When flushed it explodes from underfoot in a fast, erratic, zigzagging flight that historically made it the most challenging of all quarry species for hunters — giving rise to the word 'sniper'. In spring, male snipe perform 'drumming' or 'bleating' display dives over their territories: as the bird stoops at speed, the two outermost tail feathers spread and vibrate in the airstream to produce a distinctive pulsing sound audible for hundreds of metres.

children.name: Gallinago gallinago
children.description: The common snipe is a small, stocky wader native to the Old World, where it breeds in marshes, bogs, tundra and wet meadows. It is usually shy and well-camouflaged, foraging in soft mud mainly for insects and earthworms but also some plant material. During courtship, males perform a "winnowing" display; flying high in circles and then taking shallow dives to produce a "drumming" sound by vibrating the tail feathers.

children.name: Lymnocryptes
children.description: Jack snipe — a monotypic genus containing only the jack snipe, the smallest member of the Scolopacidae and one of the most secretive and camouflaged of all waders. Sometimes included within Gallinago, Lymnocryptes is treated as distinct based on its unique morphology, behaviour, and molecular data. The jack snipe has a distinctly different tail structure from Gallinago snipe, with a pointed central tail and different outer tail feather morphology. Its extreme reluctance to flush and habit of 'freezing' motionless in vegetation has made it one of the most challenging waders to study.

children.name: Lymnocryptes minima
children.description: The world's smallest snipe and one of its most cryptic birds, relying entirely on camouflage and freezing behaviour rather than flight for predator avoidance. Breeds in boggy subarctic forest from Scandinavia to Siberia; winters south to tropical Africa and South Asia. Males perform a remarkable ground display resembling the galloping of a horse — a repetitive four-beat sound produced mechanically — giving rise to one of its folk names 'horse snipe'. The bouncing feeding action caused by compressed body movements is highly distinctive.

children.name: Coenocorypha
children.description: New Zealand snipe — a genus of small, cryptic snipe endemic to the remote islands of New Zealand and its subantarctic territories. These birds evolved in isolation following colonisation of the southwestern Pacific by a Gallinago ancestor, diverging into several island-specific forms. Coenocorypha snipe are characterised by their extremely short wings and almost flightless habits on their predator-free island homes; several island populations were extirpated following the introduction of rats and other predators. The surviving species perform remarkable nocturnal aerial display flights, producing sounds with their outer tail feathers — a behaviour paralleling the 'drumming' of their Gallinago relatives.

children.name: Scolopax
children.description: Woodcocks — the most terrestrial of the sandpipers, inhabiting damp woodland and forest rather than open wetlands. The Eurasian woodcock is one of the most enigmatic birds in the temperate Holarctic: its cryptic dead-leaf plumage makes it essentially invisible on the forest floor, and it is almost exclusively nocturnal. The one exception is the spectacular 'roding' display flight performed by males at dusk and dawn throughout spring: the male follows a regular circuit over the woodland canopy, uttering a series of deep grunting 'orr-orr-orr' sounds alternating with a high squeaky 'twick'. Woodcocks have eyes positioned high on the sides of their head, giving 360-degree vision — a complete field of view without turning their head.

children.name: Scolopax javaensis
children.description: A poorly known woodcock endemic to the island of Java in Indonesia, inhabiting montane forest at elevations above 1,000 m in the volcanic highland interior. One of the least-studied of all woodcocks; very few confirmed observations exist and most of what is known about the species comes from specimens collected in the 19th and early 20th centuries. Like other island-endemic Scolopax woodcocks, it is classified as Vulnerable due to ongoing deforestation in its restricted range.

children.name: Scolopax scutulata
children.description: A large, dark woodcock of montane forest in the Greater Sundas and adjacent islands of Indonesia, closely related to the Eurasian woodcock but considerably larger and darker in plumage. Inhabits dense primary and secondary montane forest above 1,200 m in Sumatra, Borneo, and the Philippines. One of the more imposing woodcock species but poorly known due to its nocturnal habits and dense forest habitat; its roding display flights have rarely been described.

children.name: Tringa
children.description: Shanks, tattlers, and their allies — medium-sized, long-legged waders of wetland edges and mudflats that are among the most familiar waders in the Holarctic. The group contains some of the most abundant breeding waders in Scandinavia (redshank, wood sandpiper) as well as common passage migrants. Tringa sandpipers are characteristically nervous and alert birds — the redshank in particular functions as a sentinel species in European wetlands, its loud, piping alarm calls instantly alerting every bird in the area to potential predators. Many Tringa species breed in Scandinavia and pass through Europe in large numbers during autumn migration, when estuary mudflats and lake shores can be covered in feeding flocks.

children.name: Tringa nebularia
children.description: The common greenshank is a wader in the large family Scolopacidae, the typical waders. The genus name Tringa is the Neo-Latin name given to the green sandpiper by Aldrovandus in 1599 based on Ancient Greek trungas, a thrush-sized, white-rumped, tail-bobbing wading bird mentioned by Aristotle. The specific nebularia is from Latin nebula "mist".

children.name: Tringa glareola
children.description: The wood sandpiper is a small wader belonging to the sandpiper family Scolopacidae. A Eurasian species, it is the smallest of the shanks, a genus of mid-sized, long-legged waders that largely inhabit freshwater and wetland environments, as opposed to the maritime or coastal habitats of other, similar species.

children.name: Actitis
children.description: Common sandpiper and spotted sandpiper — two small, distinctive waders defined by their habit of constantly bobbing the rear of their body up and down as they walk. The common sandpiper is one of the most widespread and familiar waders in the Palearctic, found on the banks and shores of virtually every lake, river, and stream. Both species are characterised by a stiff-winged, low flight interspersed with glides on bowed wings just above the water surface. The two species are closely related but have complementary distributions: hypoleucos in the Old World, macularius in the Americas.

children.name: Calidris
children.description: Stints, sandpipers, and allies — the most diverse genus of small waders, encompassing the majority of the world's diminutive shorebirds and including several larger species such as the knot and the ruff. Calidris species are primarily known as passage migrants and winter visitors to coasts, with a few species breeding in Arctic and subarctic zones. The dunlin is the most abundant small wader on European coasts outside the breeding season; in autumn it forms huge, densely packed flocks that wheel and turn over tidal flats in perfectly synchronised murmurations. Many Calidris species undergo dramatic seasonal plumage changes, appearing strikingly different in breeding and non-breeding seasons.

children.name: Calidris alpina
children.description: The dunlin is a small wader in the genus Calidris. The English name is a dialect form of "dunling", first recorded in 1531–1532. It derives from dun, "dull brown", with the suffix -ling, meaning a person or thing with the given quality.

children.name: Calidris pugnax
children.description: The ruff is a medium-sized wading bird that breeds in marshes and wet meadows across northern Eurasia. This highly gregarious sandpiper is migratory and sometimes forms huge flocks in its winter grounds, which include southern and western Europe, Africa, southern Asia and Australia.

children.name: Arenaria
children.description: Turnstones — two species of stocky, short-billed waders named for their habit of flipping over stones, seaweed, and other debris with rapid sideways movements of the bill to expose small invertebrates sheltering beneath. The ruddy turnstone is one of the most widespread and cosmopolitan waders in the world, wintering on rocky shores on virtually every coastline from the Arctic to the Antarctic. Turnstones are highly adaptable feeders, known to eat carrion, birds' eggs, and even human food when other prey is scarce. The bold black-and-white and chestnut patterning of the breeding plumage ruddy turnstone is one of the most striking of any wader.

children.name: Phalaropus
children.description: Phalaropes — three remarkable species that are unique among waders in being strongly pelagic outside the breeding season, spending months on the open ocean. They have lobed toes for swimming, and are famous for their reversed sexual roles: females are more brightly coloured than males and compete for mates, while males perform all incubation and chick-rearing duties. Phalaropes feed by spinning rapidly on the water surface, creating a small vortex that draws invertebrates upward. The red-necked phalarope is one of the most abundant and wide-ranging waders in the world, with populations of millions.

children.name: Limnodromus
children.description: Dowitchers — three species of medium-sized, long-billed waders named for their distinctive feeding action of plunging the bill vertically into soft mud in rapid, repeated sewing-machine motions. They combine the elongated bill of a snipe with a plump body and medium-length legs. The two North American species, long-billed and short-billed dowitchers, are among the most challenging identification problems in shorebird watching, being nearly identical in appearance but separable by call. The Asian dowitcher is the most range-restricted of the three, breeding in central Asia and migrating to Southeast Asia.

children.name: Xenus
children.description: Terek sandpiper — a monotypic genus containing a single distinctive species characterised by its strongly upturned bill and remarkable forward-tilting feeding posture. The terek sandpiper is one of the most active and energetic of all waders, running rapidly along mudflats with its body tilted forward and bill angled upward, in pursuit of fiddler crabs and other small invertebrates. Its name Xenus means 'stranger' or 'alien' in Greek, referring to its unusual bill shape. Despite its odd morphology it is clearly part of the Tringa–Actitis clade within the Scolopacidae.

children.name: Bartramia
children.description: Upland sandpiper — a monotypic genus containing one of the most distinctive of all shorebirds, notable for its preference for dry upland grasslands rather than wetlands, its small dove-like head on a long neck, and its haunting, liquid whistling calls. Named after John Bartram, the pioneering American botanist. The upland sandpiper is one of the most important migratory landbirds of the North American interior, undertaking a remarkable migration between its grassland breeding grounds and the South American pampas.

children.name: Tryngites
children.description: Buff-breasted sandpiper — a monotypic genus (now often included within Calidris) containing a single distinctive sandpiper of dry tundra and grasslands. Sometimes treated as part of Calidris based on molecular phylogenetics, but retained here as a traditional genus. The species is famous for its extraordinary lek display behaviour and for the dramatic sexual dimorphism in size — males considerably larger than females. A bird of open landscapes, it breeds on dry Arctic tundra and winters on the pampas of South America.

children.name: Prosobonia
children.description: Pacific sandpipers — a genus of enigmatic small sandpipers restricted to remote Pacific islands, representing the outcome of ancient colonisation events from the wider Scolopacidae. Two living species are recognised, both found in the Tuamotu and Kiribati archipelagos; additional species are known from fossil records on other Pacific islands. These birds evolved in isolation on oceanic atolls, developing adaptations for foraging in coral reef and beach environments far from the continental wetland habitats typical of most scolopacids.
